answer.text: Risk-adjusted return — excess return per unit of total risk (standard deviation)
Only the downside risk of a portfolio

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Sharpe Ratio = (Portfolio Return - Risk-Free Rate) / Standard Deviation. It measures excess return per unit of total risk. **The Trap:** Sharpe uses total risk (SD), not downside only (that's Sortino). It's a risk-adjusted measure, not absolute. **Cross-Exam Context:** Tested in NISM XV Ch 12 and Series X-B. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Higher Sharpe ratio = better risk-adjusted performance. Compare Sharpe ratios to evaluate whether higher returns come with proportionally higher risk.
